exemplar.title: To what extent does urbanization impact the avian diversity and native population in the United States Midwestern ecosystems?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent does urbanization impact the avian diversity and native population in the United States Midwestern ecosystems?

subject.title: Biology
subject.description:
**IB Biology** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **four interconnected themes: Unity and Diversity, Form and Function, Interaction and Interdependence, and Continuity and Change**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, scientific inquiry, data analysis, and practical investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice and data-based questions | 1 hour 20 min | 2 hours | 36% |
| **Paper 2** | Short and extended response questions | 1 hour 30 min | 2 hours 30 min | 44% |
| **Scientific Investigation** | Individual research project | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Topic is communicated accurately and effectively with comprehensive ecological explanation
Research question is clearly stated, narrowly focused and sustained throughout
Methodology is wellâmatched to the question with clear rationale and appropriate range of methods
weaknesses: Minor verbosity and repetition in sections prevented perfect conciseness
Operational definition of âextentâ remains somewhat vague
Some methodological details (e.g., equipment specs, habitatâtype quantitative boundaries) require tightening for full reproducibility
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments all pointed to topâband performance (5/6) in communication, question focus and methodology. Despite small clarity issues (operationalization of 'extent', minor repetitions), the work meets the descriptor ââcommunicated accurately and effectively with comprehensive explanation.ââ Awarding 6 reflects its generally exemplary execution in Focus and Method.

strengths: Consistent and accurate use of subjectâspecific terminology and concepts
Integration of key studies (BDFFP, Blair 2002) provides relevant theoretical framing
weaknesses: Application of some source material is intermittent rather than continuous
Misclassification of Brown-headed Cowbird and unclear units for ARBD and PII weaken interpretability
Some citations (e.g., Pearsonâs r thresholds) lack explicit references
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While terminology use is securely in the 5â6 band, source application is ââmostly relevant and partially effective,ââ aligning with the 3â4 band (top). Persistent weaknesses around species categorization, unit clarity and citation practice justify a holistic score of 4.

strengths: Robust quantitative analysis directly linked to the research question, with clear identification of outliers
Well-structured, coherent argument threaded from introduction through conclusions
Strong real-world engagement by linking findings to urban planning exemplars
weaknesses: Discussion occasionally overstates causation from correlational data
Lack of formal inferential tests (e.g., ANOVA) and inconsistent equation formatting
Conclusions overgeneralize beyond the study region without sufficient caveats
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Analysis, evaluation and argumentation consistently perform in the 7â9 band. Although formal statistical testing and causal caveats are needed for the 10â12 band, the workâs depth, relevance and coherent synthesis justify a high mark of 9 in Critical Thinking.

strengths: Clear overall structure (abstract, intro, methods, results, discussion, conclusion) with correctly embedded figures and tables
Consistent formatting conventions: title page, TOC, page numbering, labeled graphics
weaknesses: Some low-resolution images and occasional misaligned captions
Inconsistent Table of Contents formatting and slightly informal narrative tone in places
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation meets all topÂ­band descriptors for structure and layout/formatting. Minor issues with image quality and caption alignment are insufficient to detract from an otherwise exemplary presentation, meriting the full 4 marks.

strandSelections.reasoning: The essay follows EE conventions: abstract, intro, methods, results, discussion, conclusion, references.  Figures and tables are embedded and referenced.  Although some sub-sectioning is dense, overall structure is appropriate and consistently maintained, matching the top band for Presentation.

Title page, word count, numbered pages, TOC, and figure labels are present; formatting of tables/graphs is largely clear.  A few images are low-resolution and occasional mis-aligned captions appear, but layout considerations are generally correct and complete, warranting full marks in the 3-4 band.

A coherent argument runs from introduction through results to conclusion, integrating data and literature.  Section headings, transitional sentences and hypothesis framing give strong logical flow, with only minor digressions.  This fits âclear and coherent with minor inconsistenciesâ, justifying an 8.

The student states a clear, discipline-appropriate focusâurbanisation effects on Mid-western avian diversityâand explains the ecological context, variables and expected mechanisms with appropriate biological detail. The introduction, abstract and later sections consistently reinforce this focus, demonstrating accurate and thorough communication. Minor verbosity and some repetition prevent the work from reaching the very top of the band, but the breadth and precision fully satisfy the âaccurately and effectively communicatedâ descriptor.

Key literature (BDFFP, Blair 2002, edge-effect studies) is cited to frame and interpret findings; graphs and tables are linked back to these sources in discussion.  Nevertheless, integration is periodic rather than continuous, and some citations are formulaic.  Application is therefore âmostly relevant and partially effectiveâ, aligning with the 3-4 band, top of range.

The student analyses quantitative data with graphs, SEM bars and Pearson correlations, linking patterns back to hypotheses and ecological theory.  Outliers are identified and their impact considered.  Analysis is mostly sound and clearly aligned to the RQ, though some statistical choices (small n, PCC thresholds) are not fully critiqued, preventing âexcellentâ status.  Quality matches the middle of the 7-9 band.

The methodology describes site selection, point-count protocol, patch-size calculation and building-density metrics, supported by figures and rationale for each choice.  Primary fieldwork is complemented by appropriate secondary sources.  Although replication (three surveys/site) and some controls are limited, the design is still well matched to the RQ and demonstrates deliberate, reasoned selectionâfully meeting the top-band descriptor except for slight limitations in justification depth.

Discussion links results to both null and alternative hypotheses, explores ecological mechanisms, and includes a limitations paragraph.  Conclusions are generally supported by evidence, yet some claims about plannersâ roles and âoptimalâ values are somewhat speculative.  Evaluation is therefore good but not consistently rigorous, meriting the lower end of the 7-9 band.

The research question is explicitly stated (âTo what extent does urbanisation impactâ¦â) and is narrowed to a specific region (Black Hawk County, Iowa) and clearly defined dependent variables (species richness, SDI, PII).  It is neither overly broad nor ambiguous, showing an appropriate and sustained focus.  A minor weakness is that âextentâ could have been operationalised more sharply (e.g. specifying statistical thresholds), hence a 5 rather than 6.

Terminology such as âfragmentationâ, âisolationâ, âSimpsonâs Diversity Indexâ, âpercentage of invasive individualsâ and âedge effectâ is used accurately and consistently throughout. Occasional stylistic lapses (âedgeâ effectââ) are minor, so performance fits securely in the 5-6 band at mark 5.

annotations.comment: The research question is clear but 'extent' remains vague; specify measurable thresholds or statistical criteria to operationalize it.
Abstract effectively summarizes methodology and key findings, linking habitat gradient to bird richness and invasive prevalence.
Abstract recommendations imply causation from a correlational study; revise language to reflect associative results.
Habitatâtype categories lack quantitative boundaries; define precise metrics (e.g., patch area ranges) for reproducibility.
Table 1âs qualitative habitat descriptions impede method replication; include numerical thresholds for forest, prairie, residential.
Specify the mapping resolution and error margin when measuring patch size to clarify the precision of fragmentation data.
The materials table lists 'Watch' without model or specifications; clarify equipment details to ensure methodological transparency.
The title concisely conveys the investigationâs focus on urbanization and bird diversity, ensuring immediate clarity.
The opening narrative is vivid but leans toward dramatic description; streamline for academic tone and focus.
Integration of BDFFP and Blair (2002) studies is well executed, providing relevant background for hypotheses.
The choice of a 300 m expansion radius for building densities requires citation or justification to support its ecological relevance.
Classifying Brown-headed Cowbird as invasive misrepresents a native brood parasite; revisit categorization and citations.
Table 1 presents raw species counts without inferential statistics; include tests (e.g., ANOVA) to support claims of significance.
Inferring significance from non-overlapping SEM is misleading; perform and report formal statistical tests to validate differences.
Equation formatting is inconsistent; present Simpsonâs Index as $$D=1-\frac{\sum n(n-1)}{N(N-1)}$$ for clarity and consistency.
PII values in the invasive species table vary widely; clarify whether percentages represent perâsurvey or aggregated prevalence.
Conclusion overgeneralizes beyond Midwestern context; constrain recommendations to the regions and conditions studied.
References list shows inconsistent styling and lacks hanging indents; apply a uniform citation format for clarity.
URLs in reference section break across lines, reducing readability; consolidate each link into a single line or hyperlink.
ARBD values exceed 100 without unit explanation; specify units or recalculate density to ensure interpretability.
Pearsonâs r equation lacks contextual citation; explicitly reference Haese et al. when introducing correlation criteria.
Correlation threshold table omits source and clarity on |r| boundaries; label criteria consistently and cite origin.
Outlier exclusion (R2, DT3) requires explicit statistical criteria and discussion of its influence on correlation values.
The discussion implies causation from correlations between building density and invasive species; add a caveat on correlational limits.
Linking Singaporean urban planning examples to findings demonstrates strong critical engagement with real-world applications.
Table of Contents formatting is inconsistent; align entries and ensure clear demarcation between sections and page numbers.
